ORDER (1) REGARDING MOTION FOR DISQUALIFICATION, AND
(2) CONTINUING STAY OF SANCTIONS AND CASE PROCEEDINGS
Dr. Scott seeks review of sanctions imposed against him under the
Horseracing Integrity and Safety Act, which include a period of Ineligibility, a fine,
and a cost assessment.1 On March 10, 2026, he filed what was entitled an
“Unopposed Application for Stay” of the sanctions. In response, the Horseracing
Integrity and Safety Authority (the “Authority”) explained that its non-objection to
the stay motion was based, in summary, on matters relating to an undisclosed
ex parte communication between Dr. Scott and his counsel and the Arbitrator
during a break in the arbitration hearing that has given rise to this review
proceeding.2 Thus, the Authority’s non-opposition to a stay was conditioned on
either: “(i) vacatur of the Arbitrator’s decision and remand for new arbitration
proceedings before a new arbitrator, 16 C.F.R. § 1.146(d)(3)(i); or (ii) fulsome, de
1 Dr. Scott’s Unopposed Application for Stay, Ex. A, at 35. See generally 15 U.S.C. §§ 3051 et seq.
2 Authority’s Response to Appellant’s Application for Stay at 2-3.

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/24/2026 OSCAR NO. 615103 -PAGE Page 2 of 3 *PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
novo review by the ALJ, including ‘determination de novo’ of whether Appellant is
liable for the Anti-Doping Rule Violations charged, id. § 1.146(b).”3
In these circumstances, I issued an Order, which in relevant part directed
Dr. Scott to submit a notice: (1) “stating whether or not he object[ed] to an Order: (a)
vacating the Arbitrator’s decision . . . and the sanctions” against him and (b)
remanding this case to JAMs for new arbitration proceedings before a new
arbitrator”; or (2) if he objected to that relief, “stat[ing] the grounds for his objection
and . . . , if he wishes, any other [proposed] course of action.”4
On March 23, Dr. Scott sent the Office of Administrative Law Judges an
email explaining reasons for objecting to my assignment to this case and requesting
“re-assignment to a different ALJ,” or failing re-assignment, “an opportunity to brief
the issue of disqualification. . . . Notwithstanding this objection, Dr. Scott will
comply with the [March 17] order for briefing on the proposed order and will timely
file the required notice.”
Insofar as Dr. Scott seeks my disqualification, that should take priority over
other matters, however.
Accordingly, it is hereby ORDERED that:
1. If Dr. Scott seeks my disqualification, he should FILE an appropriate
motion on or before 5:00 p.m. ET on April 3, 2026. FTC Practice Rule 4.17, although
covering Disqualification of Commissioners, may be consulted for guidance. The
3 Id. at 3.
4 March 17, 2026 Order (1) Directing Further Papers on the Application for Stay, and (2) Staying
Sanctions and Case Proceedings, at 6 (¶ 1) (the “March 17 Order”).
2

FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION | OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY | FILED 03/24/2026 OSCAR NO. 615103 -PAGE Page 3 of 3 *PUBLIC *
PUBLIC
motion should be supported by affidavits and other information, including legal
authorities and ethical standards, setting forth with particularity the alleged
grounds for disqualification. Courtesy copies must be contemporaneously emailed to
OALJ@ftc.gov and to counsel for the Authority.
2. Except as to papers to be filed in connection with Dr. Scott’s intended
motion for disqualification, the STAY of the sanctions imposed, and all further
proceedings in this review, directed in paragraph 4 of the March 17 Order, is
CONTINUED in full force and effect pending further Order of the Court. This stay
includes, but is not limited to, all papers provided for in paragraphs 1-3 of the
March 17 Order.
Jay L. Himes
ORDERED:
Jay L. Himes
Administrative Law Judge
Date: March 24, 2026
3
doc_title: Order (1) Regarding Motion for Disqualification, and (2) Continuing Stay of Sanctions and Case Proceedings
